name: Chandler Mt.
short_description: Public BLM land near Sweet Creek offering excellent rockhounding for agate, jasper, and quartz specimens. Gentle slopes and accessible terrain make this a rewarding site for collectors of all skill...
description:
Chandler Mt. is a popular rockhounding destination situated on public BLM land in Linn County's scenic Cascade foothills region. The site sits at approximately 2,097 feet elevation with gentle slopes that make access manageable for most rockhounders. The area's geology reflects the complex volcanic and hydrothermal activity that shaped the Oregon Cascades, resulting in good deposits of agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens throughout the hillsides and creek beds.

This location is particularly valued for its accessibility and the quality of specimens found here. Rockhounders regularly find colorful agate nodules and jasper pieces scattered across the gentle terrain, especially after seasonal weathering exposes fresh material. The mixed agate and jasper specimens often display banded patterns and attractive coloration typical of Cascade volcanic formations.

The site offers a moderate collecting experience - you will need to hike the slopes and navigate rocky terrain, but the gradient is not steep and parking is reasonably close to productive areas. Best results come from working the hillsides where erosion has exposed mineralized zones and from checking dry wash areas where specimens accumulate naturally.

As BLM public land, Chandler Mt. requires no permit for personal use rockhounding and remains open year-round. The gentle slope terrain combined with good specimen quality makes this an excellent choice for family groups and rockhounders new to the Oregon Cascades region.
history: Chandler Mt. sits within the Cascade Range volcanic province where Eocene to Miocene volcanic activity, combined with hydrothermal alteration, deposited silica-rich fluids that crystallized into agate, jasper, and chalcedony nodules throughout the region. This area has been actively rockhounded for decades due to the abundance of accessible specimens and the manageable terrain.
tips: Search the gentle hillsides and eroded banks for half-buried agate nodules - they often weather out of the surrounding rock and are easier to spot after rain or seasonal freeze-thaw cycles. Look for the characteristic banded patterns and translucent edges typical of high-quality Cascade agates and jaspers. Bring a small hand pick or chisel to extract specimens from host rock, and work the dry washes where specimens naturally concentrate. Focus effort on areas showing exposed mineralized zones where the volcanic host rock is weathered and fractured.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 2,097 feet with gentle slopes throughout the collecting area. Terrain is mixed volcanic rock and gravelly soil with scattered vegetation. Walking surfaces are uneven and rocky but not steep. Footing requires sturdy footwear but no climbing or scrambling is necessary. Parking pullouts are relatively close to productive collecting areas.
best_season: May - September. Winter and early spring bring heavy rainfall and snow at elevation that can make access difficult. Late spring through early fall provides stable weather, lower water levels in washes, and optimal ground conditions for spotting exposed specimens.
written_directions: From Sweet Creek, head east on Linn County Road 114 toward the Cascade foothills. Continue approximately 8-10 miles to BLM access points near Chandler Mt. Look for small pullouts on the north side of the road in the gentle slope areas. GPS coordinates 44.340338, -122.697622 will direct you to the main collecting area. High-clearance passenger vehicles can access pullouts, but 4-wheel drive is recommended during wet season.
road_conditions: Linn County Road 114 is paved but narrow in sections. Pull-off areas are unpaved and may be muddy in wet season. Passenger vehicles can access main pullouts year-round, though higher clearance helps during rainy months.
parking_notes: Several small dirt pullouts exist along the access road near productive collecting areas. Parking is informal - pull well off the main road. No formal parking lot, but pullouts accommodate 3-5 vehicles each.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No collecting in sensitive riparian areas. Pack out all trash. Leave no trace.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA

No motorized excavation

Pack out all trash
Leave no trace
nearest_services: Sweet Creek (approximately 10 miles west) has limited services. Albany (approximately 25 miles west) offers full services including gas, food, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved pullouts lead to collecting areas with short walks of 0.25-0.5 miles. Uneven rocky footing requires sturdy hiking boots. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Not suitable for those with significant mobility limitations, but manageable for most hikers with basic fitness.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find at Chandler Mt. in Oregon?
faq.answer: Chandler Mt. is known for colorful banded agates, red and brown jasper, and chalcedony specimens typical of Cascade volcanic formations. Most specimens found here are small to medium pieces (1-3 inches) with translucent banding and attractive natural coloration. You may occasionally find larger nodules still partially embedded in host rock.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Chandler Mt. on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on BLM land at Chandler Mt. You can collect year-round under FLPMA guidelines. The only requirement is respecting the 25 lb daily limit and following Leave No Trace principles. No fees apply.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Sweet Creek and Chandler Mt.?
faq.answer: May through September offers the best conditions. Winter brings heavy rain and snow that make the access road and collecting slopes muddy and slippery. Spring snowmelt can cause flash flooding in washes. Summer and early fall provide stable weather, lower water levels, and optimal ground conditions for spotting exposed specimens.

faq.question: Is Chandler Mt. a good beginner rockhounding site in Linn County?
faq.answer: Yes, Chandler Mt. is excellent for beginners. The gentle slopes require minimal hiking ability, specimens are relatively easy to spot and extract, and no special permits or advanced skills are needed. The variety of gem types available makes it rewarding for first-time rockhounders exploring Oregon Cascade locations.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring to rockhound at Chandler Mt.?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand pick, chisel, or pry bar to extract specimens from host rock. A 5-gallon bucket helps carry your finds (25 lb limit per day). Wear sturdy hiking boots for the rocky terrain, bring water and sun protection, and use a GPS unit or smartphone to mark productive areas. A loupe helps identify specimen quality on-site.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and dogs to rockhound at Chandler Mt. near Albany, Oregon?
faq.answer: Yes. Chandler Mt. is family-friendly and suitable for children ages 6 and up. Dogs are welcome on BLM land. The gentle slopes make it accessible for families with basic hiking ability. Just ensure children stay close, avoid loose rock hazards, and practice Leave No Trace principles by packing out all trash.
